channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: CAPHRA warns WHO against one-size-fits-all nicotine pouch restrictions
item.description: The Coalition of Asia Pacific Tobacco Harm Reduction Advocates supports WHO concerns about youth marketing of nicotine pouches but warns against overly restrictive policies that could hinder adult access to safer alternatives. CAPHRA emphasizes the importance of balancing youth protection with adult harm reduction. They advocate for cracking down on youth-targeted promotion, implementing strict age controls, and maintaining regulated adult access while upholding strong product standards. Emphasizing the dangers of combustible tobacco, CAPHRA stresses the need for a risk-proportionate approach to regulation. The group urges WHO to focus on evidence-based guidance that prioritizes reducing harm without limiting adult choices. (Published on May 19, 2026byglobenewswire.com)

item.title: Why Prohibition Fails: The Case for Smoke-Free Regulation in Sri Lanka
item.description: Debates around nicotine regulation in Sri Lanka are increasingly focusing on the unintended consequences of prohibition-oriented policies. Critics argue that restricting access to smoke-free nicotine products may strengthen illicit markets while limiting lower-risk alternatives for people who smoke combustible cigarettes. Evidence from multiple countries continues to suggest that prohibition alone rarely eliminates demand and may instead reduce regulatory oversight, product safety, and consumer protection. The discussion reflects broader global tensions between restrictive tobacco control policies and harm reduction approaches aimed at accelerating declines in smoking-related disease and mortality. (Published on May 19, 2026byprohibitiondoesnotwork.com)

item.title: GFN Insights #17 | WHY LESS THAN 5% MEANS WOMEN ARE BEING IGNORED IN HEALTH POLICY?
item.description: Women’s health continues to be underrepresented in research, policy development, and public health decision-making despite growing evidence of persistent gender-based disparities in healthcare access and outcomes. In many low- and middle-income countries, women who use nicotine products or seek safer alternatives remain largely invisible within tobacco control and harm reduction discussions. Researchers increasingly argue that the absence of gender-sensitive data and policy frameworks contributes to barriers in access, education, and appropriate health interventions. Expanding representation of women’s experiences in public health research may be essential for developing more equitable and evidence-based health policies. (Published on May 19, 2026bygfn.tv)

item.title: Aussie smoking market warning for NZ over illegal supply
item.description: Concerns over the growth of illicit tobacco markets are increasingly influencing debates around nicotine regulation and taxation policy. Developments in Australia are being cited as a warning that highly restrictive approaches and excessive taxation may unintentionally strengthen illegal supply chains while reducing access to regulated products. Public health experts and policy analysts continue to debate how regulation can reduce smoking prevalence without creating conditions that encourage black market expansion and criminal activity. The discussion highlights broader global tensions between prohibition-oriented policies, consumer behaviour, enforcement capacity, and harm reduction approaches to tobacco control. (Published on May 19, 2026bynewsroom.co.nz)

item.title: FDA’s first fruit-flavoured vape approval exposes the flaw in America’s anti-flavour policy
item.description: The FDA authorized fruit-flavored vapes from Glas Inc, marking a shift in nicotine policy. While the decision aims to reduce youth use, critics argue it exposes regulatory failures in the US. By approving only a few products, the FDA highlights contradictions in the system, favoring cigarettes over legal vapes. Approved products use age-restricted technology, but critics warn the legal market remains too limited against the booming illicit market. Critics advocate for reforms to allow more diverse vape options for adult smokers and combat illicit trade effectively. The approval signifies a pivotal moment in nicotine policy, raising concerns about unintended consequences and the need for a more comprehensive regulatory framework. (Published on May 19, 2026byclearingtheair.eu)
